episode_title: Ludwig Siegele: Inside The Economist's AI Playbook

codes.description: Deliberately constructing organizational infrastructure to enable bottom-up AI experimentation rather than top-down mandates, reflecting an editorial philosophy that mirrors the publication's existing culture of individual autonomy.
excerpts: it would have been wrong to kind of dictate from above what type of application we should build or what we should do with this technology. So what we did, we build an infrastructure I would say to to experiment

the economist has a very Strong bottom-up culture. Yes, the editor Zanny Mittenbeddows has the last word, but otherwise it's more like that you get hired, you you have to kind of produce your stuff. It's not dictated from above. And I kind of wanted to replicate that. culture with AI.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Intensive AI training programs risk overwhelming staff, generating pushback even when the content is valuable and engagement is high.
excerpts: we did a lot of boot camps to an extent that people started complaining. I think that we had kind of three weeks, three Friday afternoons in a row, three hours of AI speeches. So so colleagues uh got a bit upset about that.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Internal AI usage policies framed in plain, permissive language rather than restrictive compliance frameworks, encouraging experimentation while relying on professional judgment.
excerpts: our internal guidelines other than than the usual stuff is don't be stupid. Yes, use the stuff, but don't be stupid. Be careful with of course certain type of data.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Negotiating enterprise-level AI tool licenses primarily as a mechanism to protect proprietary content from being used for model training, not just for functionality.
excerpts: we have a deal with OpenAI, ChatGPT, so we have an enterprise license, which basically protects us somewhat from them using our content for training. That's the important thing, kind of we're very sensitive about that.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Low-code AI pipeline tools marketed for non-technical users proved more difficult for journalists to use than initially expected, revealing a gap between tool promise and journalistic reality.
excerpts: that allows us to build pipelines, tools, experiment with those. That's a low code. environment and the idea was there that even journalists, even if you're not a techie, you can build build up data pipeline and set up some tool. I mean that proved to be a bit more difficult, but at least that was the idea.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: The effort required for individual journalists to integrate AI tools into established personal workflows is systematically underestimated, especially for veteran journalists with entrenched habits.
excerpts: the barrier to entry or the investment you have to make as an individual to kind of integrate them into your personal workflow is largely underestimated. I mean, especially journalists, once you've worked for ten years in a certain way and all of a sudden there comes this this thing, this alien thing. which may do some things better than you do and you have to integrate is difficult.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Journalists operating under constant deadline pressure lack the bandwidth to learn and adopt new tools, especially when tools require context-switching to a separate platform.
excerpts: journalists are just time poor. So if you force them to kind of move over to some buffet of tools or some other website, you lose them.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Embedding AI tools directly into existing workflows (e.g., Google Docs extensions) to minimize adoption friction, aiming for a two-click experience rather than requiring platform switching.
excerpts: what you're trying to do is integrating the tools right where they work. So a lot of people at the Economist writing Google Docs So we're building kind of tools into Google Docs if they want to kind of do a style check. The idea is to have two clicks, do it with an extension
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Building AI prototypes is fast and easy with generative AI, but moving them into production is orders of magnitude harder due to IT bureaucracy, scaling requirements, competing engineering priorities, and robustness demands.
excerpts: the problem there is then then moving from a prototype, which is pretty easily built with this technology, to kind of actually implement it and get it uh robust enough and scale it and get it through the IT bureaucracy is much more difficult.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: AI models that excel at complex reasoning tasks fail at seemingly simple editorial tasks like enforcing British English style rules, revealing the uneven capability landscape of LLMs.
excerpts: we tried to build a style guide and and you you know this from Puni. We tried that a lot and that's something you expect to work, but it doesn't. At least uh f at the Stanford standards we want

I have to say it's kind of trying to teach an American to speak British English very, very difficult. It just it's not good enough. You get results, 80% works. And 20% it doesn't and or introduces mistakes.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Text and video translation emerged as the most successful and surprisingly effective AI application, combining multiple LLMs in role-based pipelines to produce publication-ready journalistic translations.
excerpts: translation, text translation is It's not a solve problem. But I mean you can really by combining different LLMs and kind of having them play certain rules, kind of re-m and of recreating a translation shop, you can get a very good even journalistic translation, which only has to be edited.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: AI video translation with lip-sync adaptation produces results that are startlingly convincing, creating an 'uncanny' experience where journalists appear to speak languages they do not know.
excerpts: Hey Jen translates it into whatever French, German, Mandarin, and then you get a proofread and you can correct the proofread and then you generate the video. And while when they generate the videos, they also adapt the mouth movements. And that's it's quite uncanny.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: AI-powered deep research tools meaningfully compress the background research phase of journalism, turning a day-long process into a much shorter one, while still requiring critical judgment about source limitations.
excerpts: the all the varieties of deep research tools are quite useful. I mean you have to be cautious because it's only a subset of of sources they they can access. And they make mistakes, but it it does basically what you do when you you get commissioned to story that or or topic that you don't really know.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Using Google Notebook LM as a shared knowledge management tool containing five years of publication archives, enabling journalists to query how coverage and editorial positions have evolved over time.
excerpts: we've created one where you It contains five years of economist issues, kind of the entire issues as a Google Doc. And you can then kind of search in there and ask, so how has our position changed or coverage of Trump changed in the past three years or five years
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Using AI for brainstorming and headline generation not because the outputs are directly usable, but because they catalyze the journalist's own creative thinking.
excerpts: if you're stuck, if you wanna Five headlines, give me five headlines for this article is quite useful, even though rarely do you use or do I use the headline or one of the headlines proposed, but it gives you ideas as kind of a coach or or a catalyst of uh thinking catalyst.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Self-critical assessment that the industry's current AI applications (summaries, Notebook LM, etc.) are useful but fundamentally incremental, not the transformative revolution often promised.
excerpts: overall I think what we do in the industry is still very incremental. I mean if the the big AI revolution is summaries or even notebook LM and yeah it's all very useful but it's not gonna change things much and it's not gonna move the needle.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: The observation that current AI capabilities are overvalued while the long-term trajectory of automation is underappreciated, with most journalism tasks potentially automatable within years.
excerpts: I think people overestimate what they can do in the short run and probably underestimate what's gonna happen in the long run. Because I still think that over time, in a few years, a lot of what journalists do, or most journalists do, can be automated.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Historical parallel between AI's impact on journalism and Gutenberg's printing press displacing scribes, with the implication that journalists must reinvent their roles just as scribes became printers.
excerpts: I always compare it with the scribes and Gutenberg. So all of a sudden with Gutenberg, you had to put the scribes were faced with a machine that did most of what they almost all of what they did before, so they had to reinvent themselves. And I think the apprentice of Gutenberg, Peter Schaeffer, he was he was a scribe and he became a printer.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Original reporting, source cultivation, and human network-building identified as the journalistic functions most resistant to AI automation and most important to invest in.
excerpts: focus on reporting. I think the more the world gets automated, there probably will be kind of a counter movement of people trying to hide more stuff. And so there's more to research.
as things become commoditized and automated, community becomes more important. So the community with the readers I have, the network.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Creating a semi-autonomous internal startup (8-10 people) that is organizationally separate enough to move fast and avoid bureaucracy, while maintaining strong connections to editorial and commercial teams.
excerpts: we're trying to fast forward us ourselves mentally three to five years. What's the world we're gonna live in?
it's a separate team. We have hired people from outside and we'll we'll do that more, but also internally. So it's a mixed team. It's also mixed t commercial editorial team.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: AI blurs the boundary between editorial, commercial, and technical decisions, requiring organizational models where these traditionally separated functions collaborate closely.
excerpts: it's also an exercise of getting commercial and editorial work together in a way which kind of before you just had the Chinese wall, that has gone away with tech and with AI will go away even more so 'Cause it's not clear what is a tech decision, what's uh an editorial decision, what's a commercial decision.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Explicitly drawing on lessons from a failed 2007 skunkworks project to design the AI lab differently, ensuring it is not too isolated from the parent organization.
excerpts: I was part of the skunkworks at The Economist and I was burned in two thousand and seven a project called Project Red Stripe and where we kind of were ensconced in some office in the top floor of some I think it was some uh advertising agency and it was and of course this doesn't work.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Having both the CEO and editor-in-chief actively championing AI (described as even more enthusiastic than the AI lead) identified as a critical enabler of innovation.
excerpts: both the CEO of the Economist Group and the editor of the magazine are all in favor. They are in in some ways even more gung ho when it comes to AI than I am.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Fear that publishers become mere data feeds for AI aggregators, losing audience contact, content presentation control, and editorial brand as intermediaries extract value from their content.
excerpts: are we moving into a world where publishers become mere data feeds and we feed into some aggregators like perplexity or particle or what have you? open AI and they take our content and mi mash it up
we lose contact with those readers or consumers. We lose the ability to control how our content is presented
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Preferring reciprocal partnerships with tech companies (like the Notebook LM deal with Google) over simple content licensing, emphasizing mutual learning, editorial control, and content attribution.
excerpts: The beauty with the notebook deal is w that it was a give and take. So we give them their content. Yes, there's some Some money flowing, but that's not important, but we learn to work together. We have some control over how they present our content.
it's clear on Notebook LM what is our content, what we are responsible for, and what Google is what the machine produces
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Publishers are fighting and losing against increasingly sophisticated AI scrapers that circumvent blocking measures, with Economist content appearing in ChatGPT despite countermeasures.
excerpts: we try to keep the scrapers out, which m works more or less, I think increasingly less
For some sites there must be more scrapers now than than actually human visitors
we do block it, but if you go to I think OpenAI uh chat gpt you'll find our stuff and pr quite recent articles.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Emerging attempts to create marketplaces for publisher content (Tolbit, Cloudflare pay-per-crawl, ProRata) are important but uncertain, depending on legal outcomes and whether they can generate sufficient revenue.
excerpts: Google Cloudflare or Tolbit or Pro Rada, these are all attempts to create a marketplace for content, for scraping. And I think that's very, very important. Kind of that's how you how you make that work. Whether it will work, it's hard to predict.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: The argument that AI companies need fresh, high-quality journalism content to function, creating an economic interdependency that should motivate them to sustain the publishing ecosystem.
excerpts: if these model makers need fresh content, and of course traditional publishers won't be the only source of that, but if they want to have fresh content, they have to let us live. And good journalism is not cheap.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Honest admission that the organization has not established rigorous ROI measurement for AI experiments, partly by design during the experimentation phase, but increasingly recognized as necessary.
excerpts: We don't really, uh have to admit, which is not good. But I mean we started this uh one and a half a half years ago or almost two years ago now. And so if the main aim is let people experiment, you shouldn't start to kind of think about can I ever make money with that?
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Prediction that many AI features publishers are painstakingly building now (summaries, text-to-speech, etc.) will become standard features in next-generation CMSs, making custom development wasteful.
excerpts: many things we're kind of now painstakingly developing will be part of the next generation of CMSs
summaries is basically table stakes, so you have to have some summary function. Text to speech, everybody has text to speech.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Strategic principle to build in-house only AI capabilities that are editorially distinctive (semantic search, custom style guides) while buying commoditized features from vendors.
excerpts: we took broad search in-house. And so s we had a kind of I think we used Google for the search Our search engine, we had no control over what they did, didn't get the data really. So we're trying to build that ourselves, which is semantic search now
we have a style book which has, I think, 250 pages of style rules and how we spell things. So it's it's very specific to us, so it makes sense for us to build that ourselves.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Observation that AI model companies optimize for math, coding, and scientific benchmarks rather than writing quality, creating a structural misalignment with journalism's core needs.
excerpts: writing is I think not a main focus of of these companies. I mean they want to m win their math competitions or have PhD level physics, good writing is is not what they optimize for.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Running internal AI competitions open to both editorial and commercial staff as a mechanism for fostering experimentation culture and surfacing innovative use cases.
excerpts: Internal AI competition, not just not just the newsrooms, but also commercial. We did that last year. We did that this year I think we had 80 entries.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Using AI models to predict Supreme Court rulings as a novel journalistic application that combines AI capabilities with editorial judgment, moving toward agent-per-judge architectures.
excerpts: we have the appropriate prompt and you ask the thing to predict. And uh that was the first round. We're now trying to build a more sophisticated version where we kind of build an agent for each judge.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: Drawing a parallel between the current AI adoption curve and how long it took journalists to integrate Google search into their workflows circa 2000, suggesting patience is warranted.
excerpts: if you look at how journalists learned to use Google in 2000 when it kind of became a thing also took a few years. And now you you can couldn't do journalism with without a search engine.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: The Economist's institutional identity as ideologically pro-technology and pro-change reducing organizational resistance to AI adoption compared to other newsrooms.
excerpts: we have a certain ideology, we're pro-change, pro-technology. we embrace technology and would be weird for us for us journalists and kind of to say, oh no, when it comes to us we don't want to touch this stuff.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

codes.description: A proposed experiment to record editorial debate transcripts and feed them into an LLM to draft leader articles, representing a bold test of AI's ability to synthesize human deliberation into editorial output.
excerpts: the idea is to yes, let's do that. And then let's tape this and put the transcription into some model and let the model write the leader and see what happens. And uh if it's good we'll publish it.
codes.speaker: Ludwig Siegele

episode_summary: Ludwig Siegele describes The Economist's AI strategy as a deliberate experiment in bottom-up innovation infrastructure, where the publication's existing editorial autonomy culture is replicated in its approach to AI adoption. The conversation reveals a persistent gap between the ease of AI prototyping and the difficulty of production deployment, with translation emerging as the surprising standout success while the style-guide checker illustrates the jagged frontier of LLM capabilities. Siegele articulates a striking temporal paradox: current industry efforts are dismissed as incremental while the long-term trajectory points toward automation of most journalistic functions. The creation of a semi-autonomous AI lab represents an organizational bet on leapfrogging beyond incremental gains, informed by a candid reckoning with past innovation failures and the dissolving boundary between editorial and commercial functions.
surprises: Translation, not writing assistance or summarization, emerged as the most successful AI application -- a finding Siegele himself describes as surprising.

The style guide checker, expected to be a straightforward win, failed at Economist standards despite seeming like a simple rule-following task, while AI excels at far more complex tasks like predicting Supreme Court decisions.
Siegele admits The Economist has no meaningful ROI measurement framework for AI experiments, deliberately so during the experimentation phase.
The Economist does not have a training data deal with any AI model maker, partly as a strategic choice to avoid becoming a 'mere data feed.'
The 2007 Project Red Stripe failure is explicitly invoked as a cautionary tale shaping the current AI lab's design -- organizational memory directly influencing innovation architecture.
tensions: Incremental gains vs. transformative ambition: Siegele simultaneously leads incremental AI projects and criticizes the industry for being too incremental, creating an internal tension in resource allocation.
Experimentation ethos vs. ROI accountability: The deliberate choice not to measure ROI during the experimentation phase clashes with the growing organizational need to justify AI investments.
Blocking scrapers vs. acknowledging futility: The Economist invests in blocking AI scrapers while openly admitting the effort is increasingly futile and their content appears in ChatGPT anyway.

Bottom-up culture vs. adoption gaps: Despite building experimentation infrastructure, individual journalists still resist adoption due to time poverty and workflow disruption, suggesting cultural openness alone is insufficient.

Partnership with tech companies vs. disintermediation fear: The Notebook LM partnership with Google is celebrated while simultaneously fearing that such deals could make publishers into data feeds for the very platforms displacing them.
